metadata.title: Build Physics Sims with Claude Code + Manim
metadata.color_semantics: Okabe-Ito: TEAL #009E73 = good/true, CRIMSON #D55E00 = bad/broken. INK for all text. Shape fills only for TEAL/CRIMSON.
metadata.note: Medhavy register. B00 = brand intro (MedhavyOpen); B01–B12 = three simulation segments (PROMPT→SCRIPT→RUN→CHANGE); B13 = brand outro (MedhavyOutro). Pronunciation rule: narration_text sends 'Medhavy' to ElevenLabs; on-screen props show 'Medhavy'. Stop before publishing — proof cut only.

beats.narration_text: Medhavy A-I. From Sanskrit — meaning intelligent, or intellectually brilliant. The perfect name for our A-I-powered intelligent learning systems.

lines: — the perfect name for our AI-powered

beats.narration_text: The prompt that generates this sim. Claude Code terminal: claude — write a Manim scene, photoelectric effect on sodium, phi two-point-two-eight eV. Fire photons at red, green, UV. Eject only when h-nu exceeds phi; speed as root of h-nu minus phi. Three things every prompt must pin down: the rule, the numbers, what to render.

props.command:
claude "write a Manim scene:
  photoelectric effect, Na (Φ=2.28 eV)
  photons 700 / 546 / 300 nm
  eject e⁻ if hν > Φ;  speed ∝ √(hν−Φ)"

beats.narration_text: The CLI writes the file. Before you run it — read it. Find this line: max of zero, E minus PHI. That is the entire physics. One subtraction, one clip-to-zero. Check phi is in electron-volts, E is twelve-forty over lambda, the max enforces the threshold. Thirty seconds. Not optional.

props.code:
# photoelectric.py  —  Claude Code output
PHI = 2.28          # eV  sodium work function

class PhotoelectricScene(Scene):
    def construct(self):
        for lam in [700, 546, 300]:     # nm
            E = 1240 / lam              # hν in eV
            K = max(0.0, E - PHI)      # ← the physics
            if K > 0:
                v = np.sqrt(K)          # speed ∝ √K
                self.play(GrowArrow(
                    Arrow(ORIGIN, UP*v, color=TEAL)))
# 700 nm → 1.77 eV  K=0.00  blocked
# 300 nm → 4.13 eV  K=1.85  ejected  ✓

beats.narration_text: Run the sim. Two things to verify: red and green are blocked at any intensity — the BELOW THRESHOLD chip confirms it. UV ejects, and the velocity arrow scales as root of kinetic energy, shorter near threshold, longer above it. Both correct. Note it. You are about to iterate.

Iterate: claude — update photoelectric dot py — velocity arrow scales explicitly as root of h-nu minus phi, print K in electron-volts under each electron. Submit, read the diff, re-run. Prompt, read, run, check, change. The physics was the excuse to run the loop once.

props.command:
claude "update photoelectric.py:
  → arrow length ∝ √(hν−Φ)
  → print K in eV under each electron"

beats.narration_text: New segment. Prompt: claude — write a Manim scene, Compton scattering at zero, ninety, one-eighty degrees. Show photon and recoil arrows. Label delta-lambda equals lambda-C times one minus co sign theta in picometers for each case. Geometry, formula, all three cases. The more you specify, the less you correct.

props.command:
claude "write a Manim scene:
  Compton scattering at θ = 0°, 90°, 180°
  show photon + recoil arrows
  label Δλ = λ_C(1−cosθ) in pm"

beats.narration_text: Read the file. Find the formula: LAMBDA_C times one minus co sign theta. Check the constant — two-point-four-two-six picometers. Check that co sign receives radians, not degrees. The CLI usually nails the formula. It sometimes misses the unit. The read step is where you catch it before wasting a render.

props.code:
# compton.py  —  Claude Code output
import numpy as np
LAMBDA_C = 2.426e-3   # nm  (h / mₑc)

class ComptonScene(Scene):
    def construct(self):
        for theta_deg in [0, 90, 180]:
            th = np.radians(theta_deg)        # ← radians
            dl = LAMBDA_C*(1-np.cos(th))      # ← formula
            lbl = f"Δλ = {dl*1e3:.3f} pm"
            self.draw_panel(theta_deg, dl, lbl)
# θ=  0°  Δλ = 0.000 pm
# θ= 90°  Δλ = 2.426 pm  ✓
# θ=180°  Δλ = 4.852 pm  ✓

beats.narration_text: Run it. Two checks: at zero degrees, delta-lambda is zero — the photon passes unchanged. At one-eighty, delta-lambda doubles — the photon fully reverses. These are the boundary cases. If either fails, the formula is wrong. Both hold here. The collision geometry is sound. Check done.

Iterate: claude — update compton dot py — animate scattered-arrow length proportional to delta-lambda, stretched visibly for ninety and one-eighty degrees. One diff, one re-run. You are not studying Compton scattering. You are getting fast at the loop. That is a different and more durable skill.

props.command:
claude "update compton.py:
  → scattered-arrow length ∝ Δλ
  → stretch visibly for 90° and 180°"

beats.narration_text: Final segment. Prompt: claude — write a Manim scene, UV catastrophe. Planck versus Rayleigh-Jeans on the same axes. Normalise so Planck peaks at one. X-axis is h-nu over k-T, T equals three thousand Kelvin. Specify the normalisation explicitly — otherwise the curves live on different scales and the comparison tells you nothing.

props.command:
claude "write a Manim scene:
  UV catastrophe: Planck vs Rayleigh-Jeans
  normalise Planck to peak = 1
  x = hν/kT,  T = 3000 K"

beats.narration_text: Read the file. Find the Planck function: x-cubed over e-to-the-x minus one. Find the normaliser — divide by the peak value at x equals two-point-eight-two-one. No normaliser means the y-scales differ and the comparison fails. That is the one line to verify before you render.

props.code:
# blackbody.py  —  Claude Code output
import numpy as np
_PEAK = 1.419          # Planck peak at x=2.821

def planck(x):         # ← the formula
    if x < 0.001: return 0.0
    return x**3/(np.exp(x)-1) / _PEAK  # ← normalised

def rayleigh_jeans(x): # classical — diverges
    return x**2 / _PEAK

class BlackbodyScene(Scene):
    def construct(self):
        ax = Axes(x_range=[0,8], y_range=[0,7])
        ax.plot(planck,         color=TEAL)
        ax.plot(rayleigh_jeans, color=CRIMSON)

beats.narration_text: Run it. Two checks: curves agree at low x — confirmed. Rayleigh-Jeans diverges while Planck stays bounded — confirmed. At x equals six, classical exceeds Planck by over one hundred and fifty times. Infinite energy from a warm object. The machine showed exactly why classical physics could not be the end of the story.

Final iteration: claude — update blackbody dot py — add a ratio label at x equals six, Rayleigh-Jeans divided by Planck. Three simulations. Twelve beats. Prompt, read, run, check, change. That is the reel. The physics floated by. The loop is what you take with you.

props.command:
claude "update blackbody.py:
  → add ratio label at x=6: RJ/Planck = N×
  → annotate the divergence"

beats.narration_text: Medhavy. A-I-powered intelligent learning systems. On YouTube, at Medhavy A-I. Medhavy dot com.
